In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), a **member of Parliament** (**MP**) is an individual elected to serve in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Although the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") is also a house of Parliament, [its members](/wiki/List_of_current_members_of_the_House_of_Lords "List of current members of the House of Lords") are not termed "Members of Parliament".

## Electoral system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Electoral system")]

All 650 members of the UK House of Commons are elected using the [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") system in single member [constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") across the whole of the United Kingdom, where each constituency has its own single representative.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Elections")]

All MP positions become simultaneously vacant for [elections](/wiki/Election "Election") held on a five-year cycle, or when a [snap election](/wiki/Snap_election "Snap election") is called. Since the [Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022](/wiki/Dissolution_and_Calling_of_Parliament_Act_2022 "Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022"), Parliament is automatically dissolved once five years have elapsed from its first meeting after an election.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

If a vacancy arises at another time, due to death or [resignation](/wiki/Resignation_from_the_House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Resignation from the House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), then a constituency vacancy may be filled by a by-election. Under the [Representation of the People Act 1981](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1981 "Representation of the People Act 1981") any MP sentenced to over a year in jail automatically vacates their seat. For certain types of lesser acts of wrongdoing, the [Recall of MPs Act 2015](/wiki/Recall_of_MPs_Act_2015 "Recall of MPs Act 2015") mandates that a [recall petition](/wiki/Recall_petition "Recall petition") be opened; if signed by more than 10% of registered voters within the constituency, the seat is vacated and a by-election is held.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Eligibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Eligibility")]

In the past, only male adult property owners could stand for Parliament. In 1918, [women acquired the right to stand for Parliament](/wiki/Women_in_the_House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Women in the House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), and to vote.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

To be eligible to stand as an MP, a person must be at least 18 years old and be a [citizen of the UK](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law"), a [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") nation, or [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"). A person is not required to be registered to vote, nor are there any restrictions regarding where a candidate is a resident.[[8]](#cite_note-el-8)[[9]](#cite_note-el_NI-9)

The [House of Commons Disqualification Act 1975](/wiki/House_of_Commons_Disqualification_Act_1975 "House of Commons Disqualification Act 1975") outlaws the holders of various positions from being MPs. These include [civil servants](/wiki/Civil_Service_(United_Kingdom) "Civil Service (United Kingdom)"), regular [police officers](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_the_United_Kingdom "Law enforcement in the United Kingdom") (but not [special constables](/wiki/Special_constable "Special constable")), regular members of the [armed forces](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") (but not reservists), and some [judges](/wiki/Judiciaries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Judiciaries of the United Kingdom"). Members of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") were not permitted to hold Commons seats until the passing of the [House of Lords Reform Act 2014](/wiki/House_of_Lords_Reform_Act_2014 "House of Lords Reform Act 2014"), which allows retired or resigned members of the House of Lords to stand or re-stand as MPs. Members of legislatures outside of the Commonwealth are excluded,[[8]](#cite_note-el-8) with the exemption of the [Irish legislature](/wiki/Oireachtas "Oireachtas").[[9]](#cite_note-el_NI-9) Additionally, members of the [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") (Welsh Parliament) or the [Northern Ireland Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Assembly "Northern Ireland Assembly") are also ineligible for the Commons according to the [Wales](/wiki/Wales_Act_2014 "Wales Act 2014") and [Northern Ireland (Miscellaneous Provisions)](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_(Miscellaneous_Provisions)_Act_2014 "Northern Ireland (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2014") Acts respectively, passed in 2014 (but members of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") are eligible).

People who are [bankrupt](/wiki/Bankruptcy_in_the_United_Kingdom "Bankruptcy in the United Kingdom") cannot stand to be MPs.[[8]](#cite_note-el-8) The [Representation of the People Act 1981](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1981 "Representation of the People Act 1981"), excludes persons who are currently serving a prison sentence of a year or more.[[10]](#cite_note-10) People in respect of whom a bankruptcy restrictions order has effect are disqualified from (existing) membership of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") (details differ slightly in different countries).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Members are not permitted to resign their seats. In practice, however, they always can. Should a member wish to resign from the Commons, they may request appointment to one of two ceremonial Crown offices: that of [Crown Steward and Bailiff of the Chiltern Hundreds](/wiki/Crown_Steward_and_Bailiff_of_the_Chiltern_Hundreds "Crown Steward and Bailiff of the Chiltern Hundreds"), or that of [Crown Steward and Bailiff of the Manor of Northstead](/wiki/Crown_Steward_and_Bailiff_of_the_Manor_of_Northstead "Crown Steward and Bailiff of the Manor of Northstead"). These offices are [sinecures](/wiki/Sinecure "Sinecure") (that is, they involve no actual duties); they exist solely to permit the "resignation" of members of the House of Commons. The [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") is responsible for making the appointment, and, by convention, never refuses to do so when asked by a member who desires to leave the House of Commons.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Title")]

Members of Parliament are entitled to use the post-nominal initials MP. MPs are referred to as "honourable" as a courtesy only during debates in the House of Commons (e.g., "the honourable member for ..."), or if they are the children of [peers](/wiki/Peerages_in_the_United_Kingdom "Peerages in the United Kingdom") below the rank of marquess ("the honourable [first name] [surname]"). Those who are members of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)") use the form [*The Right Honourable* (*The Rt Hon.*)](/wiki/The_Right_Honourable "The Right Honourable") Name MP.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Responsibilities")]

> The first duty of a member of Parliament is to do what they think in their faithful and disinterested judgement is right and necessary for the honour and safety of Great Britain. The second duty is to their constituents, of whom they are the representative but not the delegate. [Burke's famous declaration on this subject](/wiki/Trustee_model_of_representation "Trustee model of representation") is well known. It is only in the third place that their duty to party organisation or programme takes rank. All these three loyalties should be observed, but there is no doubt of the order in which they stand under any healthy manifestation of democracy.

— [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), *Duties of a Member of Parliament* (c. 1954–1955)[[14]](#cite_note-hop-14)

Theoretically, contemporary MPs are considered to have two duties, or three if they belong to a political party. Their primary responsibility is to act in the national interest. They must also act in the interests of their constituents, where this does not override their primary responsibility. Finally, if they belong to a political party, they may act in the interests of that party, subordinate to the other two responsibilities.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) In practice, however, the third responsibility takes precedence during voting due to [a strong whipping system](/wiki/Whip_(politics)#United_Kingdom "Whip (politics)").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-news.sky/12784304-21)[[22]](#cite_note-bbc/49578000-22)[[23]](#cite_note-parliament.uk/whips-23)
